title: Markets recalibrate amid shifting currents
summary: The BIS Quarterly Review for March 2026 documents significant market rotations during late 2025 through early 2026, with investors shifting away from US large cap and growth stocks toward European and Japanese equities and emerging markets, despite underlying equity index resilience. Single-stock volatility exceeded index-level volatility, and the Swiss franc's appreciation signaled risk-off sentiment while the dollar initially depreciated in risk-on environments—though geopolitical escalation in early March reversed these trends.
key_points: US equity gains were subdued while European and Japanese equities posted stronger gains, reflecting diversification flows away from concentrated US exposure
Single-stock volatility significantly eclipsed index volatility, even as the VIX masked underlying dispersion
Software equities and private credit portfolios with software exposure faced pressure from AI-driven disruption concerns
The Swiss franc's notable appreciation signaled global risk-off episodes, while the dollar's initial depreciation aligned with typical risk-on patterns
Middle East conflict escalation in early March triggered oil and natural gas price spikes, with precious metals trading more like risk assets than safe havens
EMEs initially outperformed US equities supported by dollar weakness and carry trades, but momentum waned with geopolitical friction
Sovereign spreads in the euro area widened again after the conflict broke out, reversing earlier narrowing
Banks, energy, industrials, consumer staples and materials led sector gains in the US rotation from growth to value
why_it_matters: This BIS quarterly assessment provides institutional-grade synthesis of cross-asset dynamics and regime shifts, documenting how geopolitical tensions disrupted the equity rotation narrative and drove renewed volatility. The divergence between index and single-stock volatility matters for portfolio protection strategies, while the breakdown of gold's safe-haven status signals evolving market structure in stress scenarios. The rotation from US growth to value and international markets has implications for portfolio allocation and factor exposure strategies.
macro_context: Inflation expectations edged up during the period, leading investors to revise monetary policy expectations upward and push back the expected timing of US rate cuts. Bond yields rose and yield curves mildly flattened as a result, reversing some of the prior steepening. The conflict in the Middle East in early March intensified risk-off sentiment and drove energy prices significantly higher.
market_structure_context: The document provides structural insights on volatility dynamics: index volatility masked sharper single-stock volatility under the surface; VIX spikes from geopolitical events were relatively short-lived; the dispersion index measured gaps between individual stock movements; and M7 concentration (approaching 35% of S&P 500) meant their declines began dragging overall market indices.
important_numbers: near dotcom bubble levels (P/E ratios for broader market, though big tech ratios comparatively lower)
notes: Source is the BIS Quarterly Review, a high-trust institutional publication. Published 16 March 2026, approximately 26 days before TODAY (2026-04-11), well within the 90-day recency window. Contains comprehensive cross-asset market structure analysis with graphical supplements referenced. The source explicitly discusses market structure dynamics (volatility dispersion, rotation patterns, concentration risk) and macro catalysts (geopolitical escalation, inflation expectations, rate path revision).
